comments:
Summary
This short paper argues that current multi-category linguistic similarity datasets (e.g. MEN, SimLex-999) are ambiguous because low numeric similarity scores conflates items that are incomparable, contrastive (antonyms), or simply distant on a continuous scale. The author(s) propose collecting lists of commonalities and differences between items rather than asking for numerical similarity judgements. They claim this approach (1) avoids the ambiguity of low scores, (2) naturally yields category structure and focal points, and (3) enables richer evaluations of vector-space meaning models (e.g. soft multi-class clustering, focal-point recovery). The paper also discusses how the approach could be extended to phrase and sentence similarity, listing various respects in which sentences can be compared (prototype effects, context/head-based similarity, discourse fit, pragmatic attention attraction, etc.).
Strengths
- Motivated by well-known psychological findings (extension effect; relation between number of commonalities and judged similarity). The basic idea—eliciting explicit commonalities/differences—is intuitive and plausibly addresses the cited ambiguity. 
- The paper links cognitive literature to practical dataset construction and proposes concrete changes to evaluation (cluster/focal-point-centric rather than only pairwise correlation).
- Discussion of sentence-level similarity is thoughtful and highlights multiple relevant dimensions beyond simple lexical overlap.
Weaknesses / Major concerns
- No empirical validation or pilot data are provided. The proposal remains at a conceptual level; it is not demonstrated that collecting commonality lists at scale is feasible, reliable, or that resulting annotations improve model evaluation.
- The data collection/annotation protocol lacks sufficient detail. Key issues not addressed include: how to instruct annotators (what counts as a ‘commonality’), how to normalize varied verbal descriptions into features, aggregation strategy across annotators, and how to handle polysemy or context-dependence.
- Scalability and cost: asking annotators to list commonalities for many pairs is more time-consuming than rating similarity on a Likert scale. The paper suggests gamification or crowdsourcing but gives no concrete plan or estimates of workload.
- Inter-annotator agreement and noise: free-text lists are likely to vary greatly; the paper does not propose methods for measuring or improving agreement (e.g. controlled vocabularies, post-hoc feature clustering).
- Evaluation proposals are high-level (soft clustering, focal point recovery) but lack formal metrics or examples. How exactly should focal points be identified and scored? How to convert commonality lists into a gold standard that can be compared to model outputs? These operational details are missing.
- The manuscript as provided contains formatting glitches and some repetition which make parts harder to follow (likely an artefact of this copy), but overall the exposition is readable.
Recommendations for improvement
1. Provide a concrete annotation protocol and example annotation instances. Show how free-text commonalities are normalized into features and how empty/common lists are handled.
2. Run and report a small-scale pilot study to demonstrate feasibility: (a) cost/time per pair; (b) inter-annotator agreement; (c) example elicited commonalities and how they induce categories/focal points.
3. Define evaluation metrics formally (e.g. cluster purity/soft clustering measures, focal-point ranking metrics, methods for comparing model-derived features to human lists), and show a worked example comparing at least one model to the proposed gold standard.
4. Discuss edge cases such as polysemy, context-dependence, morphological variants, and strategies for sampling pairs to make the process tractable.
5. Clarify the sentence-level criteria (the text refers to seven criteria but they are not cleanly enumerated) and provide concrete instructions for sentence-annotation tasks.
Conclusion
The paper makes a useful and plausible conceptual contribution: eliciting human-listed commonalities could reduce ambiguity in multi-category similarity datasets and enable richer model evaluation. However, in its current form it reads as a position/proposal piece rather than a complete empirical study. To have stronger impact it needs a concrete protocol, pilot data, and defined evaluation metrics. I recommend acceptance as a short/position/poster paper (encouraging follow-up empirical work), provided the final submission cleans up presentation issues and expands on the practical annotation and evaluation details.
